race.name: UCI Gran Fondo Loutraki
race.tagline: Greece's inaugural UCI Gran Fondo qualifier combining Mediterranean coastal riding with mountain climbing on the Isthmus of Corinth

climate.description: Mediterranean climate in early November. Loutraki is situated on the Gulf of Corinth backed by the Geraneia Mountains, offering coastal conditions with potential for variable weather during the autumn season.

terrain.surface: Mixed terrain combining flat coastal sections with rolling inland climbs. The route features Mediterranean coastal riding along the Saronic Gulf, transitioning to mountain climbing in the interior before returning to sea-level finish.
features: Mixed terrain combining flat coastal sections with rolling inland climbs
The route features Mediterranean coastal riding along the Saronic Gulf, transitioning to mountain climbing in the interior before returning to sea-level finish

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 69. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 64.

course_description.character: Mediterranean Gran Fondo combining coastal and mountain terrain. The route transitions from flat seaside riding along the Saronic Gulf through inland rolling climbs in the Peloponnese interior, returning to coastal finish. Characterized by scenic beauty, moderate climbing, and well-marked professional course management.
course_description.signature_challenge: The two-part inland climb transitioning from coastal flats to interior mountains, offering sustained rolling gradient (3.1% average) with maximum elevation of 300 m above sea level. Provides legitimate climbing test while remaining accessible to diverse ability levels.
suffering_zones: Two-part inland climb to 300 m elevation with 3.1% average gradient - sustained but manageable climbing challenge
Final 10 km includes small climb approaching finish - late-race effort test

logistics.airport: Athens International Airport (ATH) - approximately 60 km away
logistics.lodging_strategy: Loutraki is a well-established tourist destination with diverse accommodation options. The Club Hotel Casino Loutraki serves as the official start/finish venue. The city offers beaches, thermal spas, restaurants, and nightlife facilities.
logistics.transport: Loutraki is located on the Isthmus of Corinth approximately 1 hour from Athens. The event is organized by the Municipality of Loutraki in collaboration with KYVERNITIS Travel and sanctioned by the Hellenic Cycling Federation. Start and finish occur at Club Hotel Casino Loutraki, which provides secure bicycle parking.

history.origin_story: Gran Fondo Loutraki was organized for the first time in 2025, establishing Greece's inaugural UCI Gran Fondo World Series event. The event is part of the prestigious UCI Gran Fondo World Series promoting 'Cycling for All' with inclusive participation across elite, junior, master, and sportive categories.
notable_moments: 2025: November 2 - Inaugural UCI Gran Fondo Loutraki held with 400+ riders from 25 countries
Women's race winner was a 9x Greek National Champion with winning time of 2:53:42
Top 25% finishers in each UCI age/gender category automatically qualified for August 2026 UCI Gran Fondo World Championships in Niseko, Japan
Event successfully integrated into UCI Gran Fondo World Series calendar as official qualifier

biased_opinion.summary: Gran Fondo Loutraki represents an excellent entry point into UCI Gran Fondo World Series racing with genuine competitive value. The inaugural 2025 event attracted 400+ international riders and delivered on its promise of combining Greek coastal beauty with legitimate climbing challenges. The automatic qualification pathway to the 2026 World Championships in Japan elevates its prestige beyond a typical regional event. However, the 112 km distance and moderate 966 m elevation gain position it as an accessible rather than ultra-challenging Gran Fondo, making it ideal for riders seeking international competition without extreme demands.

final_verdict.one_liner: A well-organized UCI World Series qualifier offering Mediterranean scenery and legitimate competition with direct pathway to world championships.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Gran Fondo Loutraki successfully launched as Greece's first UCI Gran Fondo World Series event in November 2025, establishing itself as a quality international cycling event. The 112 km route intelligently balances accessibility with challenge, featuring flat coastal sections that welcome diverse ability levels alongside rolling inland climbs that test fitness. The automatic qualification system for top 25% finishers in each age category provides meaningful competitive stakes. Professional organization by the Municipality of Loutraki and Hellenic Cycling Federation ensures world-class standards. The location's proximity to Athens and established tourism infrastructure supports international participation. For cyclists seeking UCI-sanctioned racing with genuine world championship qualification opportunities, combined with Mediterranean scenery and strong community atmosphere, Loutraki delivers exceptional value. The event's inaugural success and integration into the prestigious UCI Gran Fondo World Series calendar positions it as a must-do qualifier for ambitious Gran Fondo racers.

videos.title: UCI Gran Fondo Greece Loutraki 2025
videos.description:
Will you conquer the challenge? Join the UCI Gran Fondo Greece Loutraki on November 2, 2025—a race where every cyclist belongs. Ride 110km through Greece’s stunning landscapes, open to all levels from amateurs to masters. By Kyvernitis Travel Group.
Video Production by RGB Studios

videos.title: Racing the UCI Gran Fondo World Championship // Road to Worlds | Ep 3
videos.description:
Filmed at the 2025 UCI Gran Fondo World Championships in Lorne, Australia: the official UCI Masters Road World Championships event for amateur cyclists. This video captures what it’s like racing against some of the best age-group riders from around the world, from the brutal climbs to the chaotic descents.

No matter how ready you think you are, race day has a way of humbling you.
Months of work all came down to one ride: Gran Fondo World Champs (on the Amy's Gran Fondo course).

🏷 #Cycling #RoadCycling #GranFondoWorlds #Insta360GOUltra #Insta360AcePro2 #HeightsCycling #CycPlus #ModerateCyclist

📷 Cameras from @insta360
GO Ultra – https://www.insta360.com/sal/go-ultra?utm_term=INR0VZA
Ace Pro 2 – https://www.insta360.com/sal/ace-pro-2?utm_term=INR0VZA

🚴 Servicing and support from Heights Cycling – https://www.heightscycling.cc/

💨 Electric Pump from CYCPLUS – https://www.cycplus.com/?ref=hfrkcicm

🎵 Music from Epidemic Sound:
Find all the tracks from my videos in this playlist –
https://share.epidemicsound.com/8lgfzh/?playlist=xlf2mzt3lywqdnhq9dxdo5lbgejd9v11

👉 Follow me on Instagram and Strava
Instagram – https://www.instagram.com/moderatecyclist/
Strava – https://www.strava.com/athletes/bkpaton

Also we have a giveaway winner: @jn92s6 who guessed 69th place correctly. I will post on Instagram soon to make contact.

19:18 - Wrap-up

It starts back on Friday morning.
The last calm moment before everything

We load up the car and head down the

out.
This is probably not the best lighting

never been to the surf coast before, and
I can't believe I haven't.
This race is going to be pretty
spectacular, right? That's probably
better. So,
it's Saturday morning. It's the day

Got in yesterday. Got the bike out. All

There's a few maintenance things that
I'm going to have to look into today.

Otherwise, I think everything's okay.

Cool. All right, let's go and crush this

This is a pretty special place to have a

about
15 minutes till we start.

So, we're going to see what that's like

>> Hang on tight.
>> Nice to get a little tip. We're over the

>> Let's do this thing.
>> 5 4 3 2 1 underway.
>> Oh my god.
The bunch immediately strings [music]
right out. You can feel the cost of
starting this far back. How deep am I
going to have to go to get to the front?
You can see riders already questioning.
Do you keep chasing or do you try and

The pressure is [music] building. The
pace, the corners. Everyone's fighting

I catch one. We race Brisbane together

A solid bunch now. Everyone sharing the
work and laying down the her.
>> Come on, guys. Come on.
The further we get into the climb, the
more riders from the earlier age group

>> Come over. You're not in there.
>> You're not in there, mate. You've

>> as we approach the summit.
>> It's all right. We'll catch up.
>> There's confidence we can catch back on.
We're only 1 minute behind. With the
climb behind us, you can feel the whole
bunch exhale.
We managed to drag a decentsized group

take on water, and see if we can make
it. It doesn't take long for the
intentions to be clear. We're motivated.
We've got numbers, and the gap to the
front is small. We hit Dean's Marsh, and
the front.
Not long after, our bunch merges with a
larger group. Most of our age bracket
and the one ahead. I spot Chris Miller
in the mix. Confirmation we back off
with the leads. The pace settles for the
next 10 km. With such a big group, maybe

brief moment of panic chasing down a

out of a corner and I'm nearly off the
back.
But then the tone changes. The front
markers from the age group behind start
rolling through.
Everyone knows it's time to lift again.
After rolling through the last feed
zone, we hit the base of the second
climb. Without a recon, this one's an
unknown. I just know that it's got two
peaks and the second half is steep. The
hitters lift the tempo and the rest of
us are left to work out how to react.
Enter Rob, a Brisbane guy I recognize
from the qualifier.
>> [ __ ] robot. Let's go. It's not good.
This is the hardest part. Let's get
through it.
>> He goes to the front and I jump straight
onto his wheel. We start carving through
the field and by the second half, we've
made up almost 50 passes. This is where
the first signs of cramping start to
hit. But I know how selective this part
of the course is going to be. If you
lose touch here, it's race over. I'm
sitting around about 50th. The next 30
riders are just up the road. Every time
you think you're at the top, there's
just a little more. I know I need to
crest with the bunch. I can't afford to
get dropped now. A few of us rally and
somehow we claw back on. The bigger
bunch drags us into the descent. I'm
pretty sure there's still a group off
the front, but together we're within
striking distance. I just need to hold
my own on the way down.
>> As we pop out of the forest, the warning
signs start to line up. Hang on tight.
>> Steve's mention of the wind tunnel at
the first clearing and a rider already
in the dirt on the side of the road.
Perfectly on queue, I get hit by a gust.
Speed drops 10k an hour. Another rider
comes past, sees me drift across the
road, and I watch him wrestle with it,
too.
Moments later, a second gust. Harder
this time. My 60 mil rims turn into
sails, pushing me straight towards the
gutter. I've got no choice. Another 10
disappears. With the 20k difference, the
bunch is gone. No wheels left to hold. I
can feel my race slipping away.
Might as well smile for the camera.
At the bottom,
there's no one inside. The race is gone.
Now it's just about finishing strong
enough not to slip any further back.
A few riders bridge across and from the
small group. It's a real mix. Some from
the age group ahead, some from
bigger guys are driving deep on the
flats. I don't have that left in but I
can hold second, take a bit more room
and still push over the walls.
Everyone's doing what they can and
somehow it works.
Along the way, we collect a few extras.
Some stick, others drift. The bunch
settles into a rhythm. Each of us taking
turns just trying to hold it together.
At 1 km to go, the group comes alive
again.
A couple of early moves. I pick the
wrong wheel and get boxed in.
200 to go. I give it a squeeze.
Not much to gain, but the feeling of
sprinting at the end of a race like
this. The crowd, the speed, the sense of
achievement.
What a rush.
I crossed the line in 69. happy with my
performance. Even if I wish I told the
girls on that decision,
>> did you guys get the gaps? So there was
probably 12 left probably bottom of the
descent and then uh some racing relaxing
little breaks coming back.
>> Yeah, we worked over the European guys
are so tactical.
I just got school.
>> Who won?
>> Danish guide in the Slovenian
then the pass
dropped a bottle like about 2ks into the

and get it.
>> Um, but there's a heap of us that fell
off the bunch on the descent. Like I
just hit that wind pocket and I was like
I was like
>> yeah I had to break
>> small you were a bit lighter than me
like I
>> was how was that bottle collection
move though
>> around I was second guessing it and then
Chris was like mate you need to get your
go by
>> doesn't [ __ ] me I'm like
Coyle, you turn.
>> Yes.
>> Right. Well, that's a wrap. We made it.
>> Got that power PR. So, I'm happy about
that. Didn't place as highly as I would
have liked to have, but what are you
going to do? Uh, happy that I wasn't
dropped on any of the climbs or or any

Uh, but you know, like safety first,
right?
So, good to see the winner. Good to see
Chris. Good to see Brad. Good to see
Ryan. They all did really well. top 10s.
I need to eat something.
>> That wraps up race weekend. Huge thanks
to my family for making the trip and
putting up with all of the chaos that
comes along with it. And a big thank you
to my partners Insta3 whose cameras
captured every part of the weekend.
Links are in the description if you want
to check it out. and also height cycling
for all of the support in the leadup to
this event and getting [music] my bike
back on the road multiple times. I'll be
doing one more episode in the Road to
World series where I dig into the
details, the decisions, what worked, and
what I would change for next time. So,
make sure you subscribe so you don't
miss out. If you raced the championship,
I'd love to hear how you went. And if
you've got any tips for me on how I can
improve my descending, please drop them
in the comments below. Other than that,

videos.curation_reason: High-quality first-person race recap from UCI Gran Fondo World Championships with good production value and views over 9K.

quotes.text: It's hard to put into words what that start line means after everything leading up to it. I didn't win. Not even close. But that's not really the point. I wanted to see how far I could go lining up against some of the best masters riders in the world.

key_challenges.name: opening climb
key_challenges.description: Located near the race start in Lorne, riders need to prepare for this early challenge that sets the tone for the race.

terrain_notes.text: The descent is described as mad fun, indicating technical downhill sections that are fast and enjoyable for skilled riders.

race_day_tips.text: Do race openers the day before - 10-minute effort in zone three, couple of two-minute efforts in zone four, and maybe one or two sprints as a wakeup call for the legs.

additional_quotes.text: Every racer knows that feeling when you're waiting at oversized baggage, hoping your bike isn't the one that never shows up.
One of my brake lines didn't really enjoy the flight. So, going to have to see if I can get some tension back in there today.

rider_intel.search_text: The UCI Gran Fondo Loutraki takes place in a spectacular coastal setting near Lorne. The race features an opening climb right from the start that riders describe as a key early challenge. The course includes technical descents that are described as mad fun and fast-paced. Temperature varies significantly between locations, with it being much warmer down in Lorne compared to higher elevations. Riders recommend doing race openers the day before consisting of zone three and four efforts plus sprints. Logistics can be challenging with bike travel, and mechanical issues like brake problems may arise from flights. The coastal location provides amazing views along the surf coast, making it a visually spectacular race venue despite the physical demands.
